page: Microsoft Azure E16-8s_v3 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine belonging to the ESv3 server family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon 8272CL processor operating at 2.6 GHz, configured with 4 physical cores and 2 threads per core, yielding 8 dedicated vCPUs. The system is equipped with 128.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 16.0 GB per core. It includes 262 GB of local storage and supports Premium Storage, while containing no GPU accelerators.

Benchmark data indicates a clear performance profile. The server exhibits weak to poor CPU performance, with stress-ng single-core results in the bottom 10% and multi-core results in the bottom 25%. Geekbench single-core and multi-core scores also fall into the bottom 25% tier. However, memory subsystem performance is more robust, with memory bandwidth for both small and large blocks scoring in the middle 50% average tier. Passmark memory and database operations, along with Redis SET operations at 6.25 million operations per second, also maintain average performance.

The primary tradeoff of the E16-8s_v3 is its low compute throughput relative to its substantial memory capacity. While CPU-intensive tasks like LLM inference on medium 7B models perform poorly, the high memory density makes it qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound workloads that do not require intense CPU processing. This configuration is suitable for database operations, caching layers, and memory-intensive enterprise applications where memory capacity is prioritized over raw processing speed.
description: Microsoft Azure E16-8s_v3 is an x86_64 memory-optimized virtual machine from the ESv3 family, powered by an Intel Xeon 8272CL processor running at 2.6 GHz. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs across 4 physical cores, 128.0 GB of RAM, and 262 GB of local storage with Premium Storage capability. Benchmarks reveal a distinct tradeoff: while CPU compute performance is weak to poor, with stress-ng single-core results in the bottom 10%, memory bandwidth and database operations perform in the average middle 50% tier. This high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 16.0 GB per core makes the server qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-bound applications that do not demand high CPU throughput. It is suited for database workloads, caching systems, and memory-intensive enterprise applications.
